title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
problem_statement: Return the kth smallest value in a binary search tree, where k is 1-indexed.

examples.explanation: Inorder traversal yields [1,2,3,4], so the smallest element is 1.

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [1, 10^4]
intuition: An inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order, so the kth visited node is the answer.

approaches.name: Recursive inorder traversal
approaches.description: Traverse left subtree, visit current node, and traverse right subtree while counting nodes until k is reached.
approaches.why_not: Collecting all values into a list is easier but uses extra O(n) space.
interview_commentary: Inorder traversal yields BST values in ascending order.
Stop traversal early when the kth value is found.
Use a counter to track the number of visited nodes.

approaches.name: Iterative inorder with stack
approaches.description: Use an explicit stack to simulate inorder traversal and stop when the kth node is popped.
approaches.why_not: Recursive code is cleaner, but iterative avoids call stack overhead.
interview_commentary: The stack simulates recursion for inorder traversal.
Push left nodes until you reach the smallest value.
Pop nodes and count until the kth smallest is reached.

interview_qa.q: Why does inorder traversal give sorted BST values?
interview_qa.a: In a BST, left subtree values are smaller, so visiting left-root-right yields ascending order.
Maintain a count and return as soon as the kth node is visited.

interview_qa.q: What is the stack space complexity?
interview_qa.a: O(h), where h is the tree height.
The algorithm traverses the entire tree and returns the largest value.
